narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 46% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction stemmed primarily from your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to an average of around the 6th decile among students who passed the bar. That low ranking in your later undergraduate performance was the biggest factor pushing the model toward predicting failure, as it signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong grades over time. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.90 stood out well above the 3.24 average of those who passed, strongly influencing the model in favor of success and helping to offset some concerns. Your family income in the low bracket also nudged the prediction toward passing, since the model recognizes that students from similar backgrounds can and do succeed when other strengths align. While your LSAT score of 26.50 was notably below the 37.27 average for bar passers and your first-year decile was similarly low, these played supporting roles rather than leading the decision. Being full-time aligns with the vast majority of successful students, so it didn't raise red flags. Overall, your profile showed real strengths like that impressive undergrad GPA, but the model weighed your weaker later-year performance as the most critical risk compared to typical admitted students, whose deciles trended much higher across the board. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to the passing average, while building evidence of improved consistency in your grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program. You might also connect with mentors from similar backgrounds who've navigated bar success, as your strong GPA suggests you have the foundation to overcome these hurdles with targeted preparation. Law schools often review reapplications favorably with such improvements, so use this as motivation to strengthen those key areas.
